name: Codex Alimentarius
description: The Codex Alimentarius is a collection of internationally recognized standards, codes of practice, guidelines, and other recommendations relating to foods, food production, and food safety. Established in 1963 by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) of the United Nations, its primary purpose is to protect consumer health and ensure fair practices in the food trade. Proponents of conspiracy theories often view the Codex Alimentarius as a sinister plot by corporations and governments to control the world's food supply, restrict access to natural health remedies, and ultimately depopulate the planet. They allege that the Codex standards are designed to benefit large agricultural businesses at the expense of small farmers and consumers, limiting access to vitamins, minerals, and alternative medicines by setting artificially low permissible levels or outright banning them. They claim the Codex promotes genetically modified organisms (GMOs) and harmful additives while suppressing organic and natural farming practices. Mainstream scientific and regulatory perspectives strongly refute these claims. The FAO and WHO maintain that the Codex Alimentarius is a voluntary set of guidelines intended to harmonize food standards globally, facilitating trade and protecting consumers. They argue that Codex standards are based on scientific evidence and risk assessments, and that member countries are free to adopt or reject them. Critics of the conspiracy theories point out that the Codex does not have the force of law unless adopted by individual countries, and that its standards are often developed through a collaborative process involving governments, industry, consumer groups, and scientific experts. The debate surrounding the Codex Alimentarius highlights the complex interplay between food safety, international trade, consumer rights, and public trust in regulatory bodies.
subtitle: Global Food Standards: Conspiracy or Protection?

claims.claim: Codex aims to control the world's food supply.
claims.evidence: Codex sets international standards for food production and distribution.
claims.criticism: Codex standards are voluntary guidelines, not mandatory laws, designed to harmonize food trade and protect consumer health. Countries can choose whether or not to implement them.

claims.claim: Codex restricts access to natural health remedies.
claims.evidence: Codex sets maximum levels for vitamins and minerals, potentially limiting access to high-dose supplements.
claims.criticism: Codex standards for vitamins and minerals are based on safety assessments and aim to prevent excessive intake that could be harmful. They do not necessarily restrict access to all natural health remedies.

claims.claim: Codex promotes GMOs and harmful additives.
claims.evidence: Codex addresses the safety of GMOs and food additives, which some interpret as an endorsement.
claims.criticism: Codex standards for GMOs and food additives are based on scientific risk assessments and aim to ensure their safe use. The Codex does not inherently promote or endorse them, but rather provides guidelines for their regulation.

keyFigures.name: Dr. Matthias Rath
keyFigures.role: Prominent proponent who claims Codex is a tool for genocide.

keyFigures.name: WHO/FAO
keyFigures.role: The organizations responsible for developing and maintaining the Codex Alimentarius.

timeline.event: Codex Alimentarius Commission established by FAO/WHO.
Dietary Supplement Health and Education Act (DSHEA) in the US, often cited in relation to Codex concerns.
Continued development and revision of Codex standards, fueling ongoing debate and conspiracy theories.

sources.author: Barrett, Stephen
sources.work: The Codex Alimentarius: What It Is and Why You Shouldn't Worry About It
sources.title: Barrett, Stephen
